title: Emergency access while traveling
text: Emergency phone numbers differ by country (e.g. many regions use 112, 911, 999, or other official codes). Use the number your destination government publishes for medical emergencies. For life-threatening conditions, call local emergency services or go to the nearest appropriate emergency department. Verify current guidance on official government or tourism sites.

title: Typical care pathways while traveling
text: Non-emergency issues often start at primary care, urgent care, or a clinic. Hospitals prioritize emergencies; routine visits may require appointments or referrals depending on local rules. After-hours options vary by city. Travelers should confirm language support, payment, and acceptance by phone when possible.

title: Payment and insurance (travelers, high level)
text: Visitors are often not enrolled in local public insurance. Travelers commonly pay out of pocket and later claim through travel insurance per policy terms. Costs vary by facility, department, and tests. Request estimates when possible; carry insurance cards and identification. Educational only—not billing or legal advice.

title: Finding care abroad
text: Cities differ in hospital density, pharmacy rules, and after-hours care. Pharmacies may counsel on minor issues where regulations allow; they do not replace emergency care for serious symptoms. Prefer official government or tourism-board health pages for your destination.

title: Heat and dehydration
text: Mild heat illness: rest, fluids, shade. Seek urgent care for confusion, fainting, persistent vomiting, or worsening symptoms—especially in hot climates or strenuous travel.
